mn9:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, in Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn9:1.5: “Yes, friend,” those *bhikkhus* replied to Venerable Sāriputta.
mn9:10.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands nutriment, understands the arising of nutriment, understands the cessation of nutriment, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of nutriment—
mn9:10.3: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].
mn9:11.1: And what, friends, is nutriment, what is the arising of nutriment, what is the cessation of nutriment, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of nutriment?
mn9:11.2: These four kinds of nutriment, friends, are for the sustaining of beings that have come to be, and for the support of those seeking a new existence.
mn9:11.4: Edible food, coarse or subtle, is the first; contact is the second; mental intention is the third; consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*] is the fourth.
mn9:11.5: From the arising of *taṇhā* comes the arising of nutriment, from the cessation of *taṇhā* comes the cessation of nutriment. This very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of nutriment, that is:
mn9:11.6: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right *sati*, right *samādhi*.
mn9:12.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands nutriment, thus understands the arising of nutriment, thus understands the cessation of nutriment, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of nutriment, he completely abandons the underlying tendency to lust, dispels the underlying tendency to aversion, uproots the underlying tendency to the view and conceit, ‘I am,’ abandons blindness [*avijjaṁ*], brings forth knowledge, and here and now makes an end of dukkha—
mn9:12.2: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].”
mn9:13.1: “*Sādhu*, friend,” those *bhikkhus*, having approved Venerable Sāriputta’s words and given *anumodanā*, asked Venerable Sāriputta a further question:
mn9:13.2: “Could there be, friends, another way in which a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*]?”
mn9:14.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands dukkha, understands the arising of dukkha, understands the cessation of dukkha, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of dukkha—
mn9:14.3: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].
mn9:15.1: And what, friends, is dukkha, what is the arising of dukkha, what is the cessation of dukkha, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of dukkha?
mn9:15.2: Birth is dukkha, aging is dukkha, death is dukkha, sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, and despair are dukkha, association with the disliked is dukkha, separation from the liked is dukkha, not getting what one wants is also dukkha; in brief, the five aggregates subject to clinging [*upādāna*] [*pañcupādānakkhandhā*] are dukkha—
mn9:16.1: And what, friends, is the arising of dukkha?
mn9:16.2: That *taṇhā* which leads to renewed *bhava* [*ponobbhavikā*], accompanied by delight and lust, delighting in this and that, namely—
mn9:16.4: this is called the arising of dukkha, friends.
mn9:17.1: And what, friends, is the cessation of dukkha?
mn9:17.2: The remainderless fading away and cessation of that very *taṇhā*, the giving up, relinquishing, release, non-attachment—
mn9:17.3: this is called the cessation of dukkha, friends.
mn9:18.1: And what, friends, is the practice leading to the cessation of dukkha?
mn9:18.2: This very noble eightfold path, that is:
mn9:18.4: this is called the practice leading to the cessation of dukkha, friends.
mn9:19.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands dukkha, thus understands the arising of dukkha, thus understands the cessation of dukkha, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of dukkha, he completely abandons the underlying tendency to lust, dispels the underlying tendency to aversion, uproots the underlying tendency to the view and conceit, ‘I am,’ abandons blindness [*avijjaṁ*], brings forth knowledge, and here and now makes an end of dukkha—
mn9:19.2: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].”
mn9:2.1: “‘Right view, right view,’ friends, is what they say.
mn9:2.2: To what extent, friends, does a noble disciple have right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*]?”
mn9:2.3: “Even from far away, friends, we would come into the presence of Venerable Sāriputta to understand the meaning of this statement.
mn9:2.4: It would indeed be *sādhu* if the meaning of this statement would occur to Venerable Sāriputta himself.
mn9:2.5: Having heard it from Venerable Sāriputta, the *bhikkhus* will remember it.”
mn9:2.6: “Then, friends, listen and attend carefully. I will speak.”
mn9:2.7: “Yes, friend,” those *bhikkhus* replied to Venerable Sāriputta.
mn9:20.1: “*Sādhu*, friend,” those *bhikkhus*, having approved Venerable Sāriputta’s words and given *anumodanā*, asked Venerable Sāriputta a further question:
mn9:20.2: “Could there be, friends, another way in which a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*]?”
mn9:21.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands aging-and-death, understands the arising of aging-and-death, understands the cessation of aging-and-death, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of aging-and-death—
mn9:21.3: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].
mn9:22.1: And what, friends, is aging-and-death, what is the arising of aging-and-death, what is the cessation of aging-and-death, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of aging-and-death?
mn9:22.2: Whatever aging of those various beings in whatever order of beings, growing old, brokenness of teeth, greyness of hair, wrinkling of skin, decline of life, maturing of the *indriyas*—
mn9:22.5: Whatever passing away of those various beings from that or that order of beings, their deceasing, breakup, disappearance, dying, death, completion of time, breakup of the aggregates, laying down of the body, cutting off of the life faculty—
mn9:22.7: Thus this aging and this death—
mn9:22.9: From the arising of birth comes the arising of aging-and-death, from the cessation of birth comes the cessation of aging-and-death. This very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of aging-and-death, that is:
mn9:23.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands aging-and-death, thus understands the arising of aging-and-death, thus understands the cessation of aging-and-death, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of aging-and-death, he completely abandons the underlying tendency to lust … here and now makes an end of dukkha—
mn9:23.2: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].”
mn9:25.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands birth, understands the arising of birth, understands the cessation of birth, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of birth—
mn9:25.3: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].
mn9:26.1: And what, friends, is birth, what is the arising of birth, what is the cessation of birth, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of birth?
mn9:26.2: Whatever birth of those various beings in whatever order of beings, their being born, descent, coming forth, manifestation of the aggregates, acquisition of the dimensions [*āyatanānaṁ*]—
mn9:26.4: From the arising of *bhava* [*bhavasamudayā*] comes the arising of birth, from the cessation of *bhava* [*bhavanirodhā*] comes the cessation of birth. This very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of birth, that is:
mn9:27.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands birth, thus understands the arising of birth, thus understands the cessation of birth, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of birth, he completely abandons the underlying tendency to lust … here and now makes an end of dukkha—
mn9:27.2: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].”
mn9:29.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands *bhava*, understands the arising of *bhava* [*bhavasamudayaṁ*], understands the cessation of *bhava* [*bhavanirodhaṁ*], and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *bhava* [*bhavanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ*]—
mn9:29.3: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].
mn9:3.1: “When, friends, a noble disciple understands the unskillful, understands the root of the unskillful, understands the skillful, and understands the root of the skillful—
mn9:3.2: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].
mn9:30.1: And what, friends, is *bhava*, what is the arising of *bhava* [*bhavasamudayo*], what is the cessation of *bhava* [*bhavanirodho*], and what is the practice leading to the cessation of *bhava* [*bhavanirodhagāminī paṭipadā*]?
mn9:30.4: From the arising of clinging [*upādāna*] [*upādānasamudayā*] comes the arising of *bhava*, from the cessation of clinging [*upādāna*] [*upādānanirodhā*] comes the cessation of *bhava*. This very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *bhava*, that is:
mn9:31.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands *bhava*, thus understands the arising of *bhava* [*bhavasamudayaṁ*], thus understands the cessation of *bhava* [*bhavanirodhaṁ*], and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of *bhava* [*bhavanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ*], he completely abandons the underlying tendency to lust … here and now makes an end of dukkha.
mn9:31.2: To this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].”
mn9:33.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands clinging [*upādānaṁ*], understands the arising of clinging [*upādānasamudayaṁ*], understands the cessation of clinging [*upādānanirodhaṁ*], and understands the practice leading to the cessation of clinging [*upādānanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ*]—
mn9:33.3: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].
mn9:34.1: And what, friends, is clinging [*upādānaṁ*], what is the arising of clinging [*upādānasamudayo*], what is the cessation of clinging [*upādānanirodho*], and what is the practice leading to the cessation of clinging [*upādānanirodhagāminī paṭipadā*]?
mn9:34.2: These four kinds of clinging [*upādānāni*], friends—
mn9:34.3: clinging [*upādāna*] to sensuality [*kāmupādānaṁ*], clinging [*upādāna*] to views [*diṭṭhupādānaṁ*], clinging [*upādāna*] to rules and observances [*sīlabbatupādānaṁ*], clinging [*upādāna*] to a doctrine of self [*attavādupādānaṁ*].
mn9:34.4: From the arising of *taṇhā* comes the arising of clinging [*upādāna*], from the cessation of *taṇhā* comes the cessation of clinging [*upādāna*]. This very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of clinging [*upādāna*], that is:
mn9:35.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands clinging [*upādānaṁ*], thus understands the arising of clinging [*upādānasamudayaṁ*], thus understands the cessation of clinging [*upādānanirodhaṁ*], and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of clinging [*upādāna*] [*upādānanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ*], he completely abandons the underlying tendency to lust … here and now makes an end of dukkha—
mn9:35.2: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].”
mn9:37.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands *taṇhā*, understands the arising of *taṇhā*, understands the cessation of *taṇhā*, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *taṇhā*—
mn9:37.3: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].
mn9:38.1: And what, friends, is *taṇhā*, what is the arising of *taṇhā*, what is the cessation of *taṇhā*, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of *taṇhā*?
mn9:38.3: *taṇhā* for forms [*rūpataṇhā*], *taṇhā* for sounds [*saddataṇhā*], *taṇhā* for smells [*gandhataṇhā*], *taṇhā* for tastes [*rasataṇhā*], *taṇhā* for tangibles [*phoṭṭhabbataṇhā*], *taṇhā* for *dhammas* [*dhammataṇhā*].
mn9:38.4: From the arising of *vedanā* comes the arising of *taṇhā*, from the cessation of *vedanā* comes the cessation of *taṇhā*. This very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *taṇhā*, that is:
mn9:39.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands *taṇhā*, thus understands the arising of *taṇhā*, thus understands the cessation of *taṇhā*, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of *taṇhā*, he completely abandons the underlying tendency to lust … here and now makes an end of dukkha—
mn9:39.2: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].”
mn9:4.1: And what, friends, is the unskillful, what is the root of the unskillful, what is the skillful, and what is the root of the skillful?
mn9:4.2: Killing living beings is unskillful, taking what is not given is unskillful, sexual misconduct is unskillful, false speech is unskillful, divisive speech is unskillful, harsh speech is unskillful, idle chatter is unskillful, covetousness is unskillful, ill will is unskillful, wrong view is unskillful—
mn9:4.3: this is called the unskillful, friends.
mn9:41.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands *vedanā*, understands the arising of *vedanā*, understands the cessation of *vedanā*, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā*—
mn9:41.3: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].
mn9:42.1: And what, friends, is *vedanā*, what is the arising of *vedanā*, what is the cessation of *vedanā*, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā*?
mn9:42.3: *vedanā* born of eye-contact [*cakkhusamphassajā vedanā*], *vedanā* born of ear-contact [*sotasamphassajā vedanā*], *vedanā* born of nose-contact [*ghānasamphassajā vedanā*], *vedanā* born of tongue-contact [*jivhāsamphassajā vedanā*], *vedanā* born of body-contact [*kāyasamphassajā vedanā*], *vedanā* born of mind-contact [*manosamphassajā vedanā*].
mn9:42.4: From the arising of contact comes the arising of *vedanā*, from the cessation of contact comes the cessation of *vedanā*. This very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā*, that is:
mn9:43.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands *vedanā*, thus understands the arising of *vedanā*, thus understands the cessation of *vedanā*, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā*, he completely abandons the underlying tendency to lust … here and now makes an end of dukkha—
mn9:43.2: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].”
mn9:45.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands contact, understands the arising of contact, understands the cessation of contact, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of contact—
mn9:45.3: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].
mn9:46.1: And what, friends, is contact, what is the arising of contact, what is the cessation of contact, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of contact?
mn9:46.2: These six kinds of contact [*phassakāyā*], friends—
mn9:46.3: eye-contact, ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, mind-contact.
mn9:46.4: From the arising of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanasamudayā*] comes the arising of contact, from the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatananirodhā*] comes the cessation of contact. This very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of contact, that is:
mn9:47.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands contact, thus understands the arising of contact, thus understands the cessation of contact, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of contact, he completely abandons the underlying tendency to lust … here and now makes an end of dukkha—
mn9:47.2: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].”
mn9:49.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanaṁ*], understands the arising of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanasamudayaṁ*], understands the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatananirodhaṁ*], and understands the practice leading to the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatananirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ*]—
mn9:49.3: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].
mn9:5.1: And what, friends, is the root of the unskillful?
mn9:5.2: Greed is a root of the unskillful, hatred is a root of the unskillful, delusion is a root of the unskillful—
mn9:5.3: this is called the root of the unskillful, friends.
mn9:50.1: And what, friends, is the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanaṁ*], what is the arising of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanasamudayo*], what is the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatananirodho*], and what is the practice leading to the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatananirodhagāminī paṭipadā*]?
mn9:50.3: the eye-dimension [*cakkhāyatanaṁ*], the ear-dimension [*sotāyatanaṁ*], the nose-dimension [*ghānāyatanaṁ*], the tongue-dimension [*jivhāyatanaṁ*], the body-dimension [*kāyāyatanaṁ*], the mind-dimension [*manāyatanaṁ*].
mn9:50.4: From the arising of *nāmarūpa* [*nāmarūpasamudayā*] comes the arising of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanasamudayo*], from the cessation of *nāmarūpa* [*nāmarūpanirodhā*] comes the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatananirodho*]. This very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], that is:
mn9:51.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanaṁ*], thus understands the arising of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanasamudayaṁ*], thus understands the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatananirodhaṁ*], and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatananirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ*], he completely abandons the underlying tendency to lust … here and now makes an end of dukkha—
mn9:51.2: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].”
mn9:53.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands *nāmarūpa*, understands the arising of *nāmarūpa* [*nāmarūpasamudayaṁ*], understands the cessation of *nāmarūpa* [*nāmarūpanirodhaṁ*], and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *nāmarūpa* [*nāmarūpanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ*]—
mn9:53.3: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].
mn9:54.1: And what, friends, is *nāmarūpa*, what is the arising of *nāmarūpa* [*nāmarūpasamudayo*], what is the cessation of *nāmarūpa* [*nāmarūpanirodho*], and what is the practice leading to the cessation of *nāmarūpa* [*nāmarūpanirodhagāminī paṭipadā*]?
mn9:54.4: and the four great elements, and *rūpa* dependent on the four great elements [*upādāyarūpaṁ*]—
mn9:54.8: From the arising of consciousness [*viññāṇasamudayā*] comes the arising of *nāmarūpa*, from the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇanirodhā*] comes the cessation of *nāmarūpa*. This very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, that is:
mn9:55.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands *nāmarūpa*, thus understands the arising of *nāmarūpa* [*nāmarūpasamudayaṁ*], thus understands the cessation of *nāmarūpa* [*nāmarūpanirodhaṁ*], and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of *nāmarūpa* [*nāmarūpanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ*], he completely abandons the underlying tendency to lust … here and now makes an end of dukkha—
mn9:55.2: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].”
mn9:57.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*], understands the arising of consciousness [*viññāṇasamudayaṁ*], understands the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇanirodhaṁ*], and understands the practice leading to the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ*]—
mn9:57.3: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].
mn9:58.1: And what, friends, is consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*], what is the arising of consciousness [*viññāṇasamudayo*], what is the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇanirodho*], and what is the practice leading to the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇanirodhagāminī paṭipadā*]?
mn9:58.2: These six classes of consciousness [*viññāṇakāyā*], friends—
mn9:58.3: eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*], ear-consciousness [*sotaviññāṇaṁ*], nose-consciousness [*ghānaviññāṇaṁ*], tongue-consciousness [*jivhāviññāṇaṁ*], body-consciousness [*kāyaviññāṇaṁ*], mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*].
mn9:58.4: From the arising of *saṅkhāras* [*saṅkhārasamudayā*] comes the arising of consciousness [*viññāṇasamudayo*], from the cessation of *saṅkhāras* [*saṅkhāranirodhā*] comes the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇanirodho*]. This very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], that is:
mn9:59.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*], thus understands the arising of consciousness [*viññāṇasamudayaṁ*], thus understands the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇanirodhaṁ*], and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ*], he completely abandons the underlying tendency to lust … here and now makes an end of dukkha—
mn9:59.2: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].”
mn9:6.1: And what, friends, is the skillful?
mn9:6.2: Refraining from killing living beings is skillful, refraining from taking what is not given is skillful, refraining from sexual misconduct is skillful, refraining from false speech is skillful, refraining from divisive speech is skillful, refraining from harsh speech is skillful, refraining from idle chatter is skillful, non-covetousness is skillful, non-ill will is skillful, right view is skillful—
mn9:6.3: this is called the skillful, friends.
mn9:61.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands *saṅkhāras*, understands the arising of *saṅkhāras* [*saṅkhārasamudayaṁ*], understands the cessation of *saṅkhāras* [*saṅkhāranirodhaṁ*], and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *saṅkhāras* [*saṅkhāranirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ*]—
mn9:61.3: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].
mn9:62.1: And what, friends, are *saṅkhāras*, what is the arising of *saṅkhāras* [*saṅkhārasamudayo*], what is the cessation of *saṅkhāras* [*saṅkhāranirodho*], and what is the practice leading to the cessation of *saṅkhāras* [*saṅkhāranirodhagāminī paṭipadā*]?
mn9:62.4: From the arising of blindness [*avijjāsamudayā*] comes the arising of *saṅkhāras*, from the cessation of blindness [*avijjānirodhā*] comes the cessation of *saṅkhāras*. This very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, that is:
mn9:63.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands *saṅkhāras*, thus understands the arising of *saṅkhāras* [*saṅkhārasamudayaṁ*], thus understands the cessation of *saṅkhāras* [*saṅkhāranirodhaṁ*], and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of *saṅkhāras* [*saṅkhāranirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ*], he completely abandons the underlying tendency to lust, dispels the underlying tendency to aversion, uproots the underlying tendency to the view and conceit, ‘I am,’ abandons blindness [*avijjaṁ*], brings forth knowledge, and here and now makes an end of dukkha—
mn9:63.2: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].”
mn9:65.1: When, friends, a noble disciple understands blindness [*avijjaṁ*], understands the arising of blindness [*avijjāsamudayaṁ*], understands the cessation of blindness [*avijjānirodhaṁ*], and understands the practice leading to the cessation of blindness [*avijjānirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ*]—
mn9:65.2: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].
mn9:65.3: And what, friends, is blindness [*avijjā*], what is the arising of blindness [*avijjāsamudayo*], what is the cessation of blindness [*avijjānirodho*], and what is the practice leading to the cessation of blindness [*avijjānirodhagāminī paṭipadā*]?
mn9:65.4: Not-knowing regarding dukkha, not-knowing regarding the arising of dukkha, not-knowing regarding the cessation of dukkha, not-knowing regarding the practice leading to the cessation of dukkha—
mn9:66.1: From the arising of *āsavas* comes the arising of blindness [*avijjā*], from the cessation of *āsavas* comes the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*]. This very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], that is:
mn9:67.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands blindness [*avijjaṁ*], thus understands the arising of blindness [*avijjāsamudayaṁ*], thus understands the cessation of blindness [*avijjānirodhaṁ*], and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of blindness [*avijjānirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ*], he completely abandons the underlying tendency to lust, dispels the underlying tendency to aversion, uproots the underlying tendency to the view and conceit, ‘I am,’ abandons blindness [*avijjaṁ*], brings forth knowledge, and here and now makes an end of dukkha—
mn9:67.2: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].”
mn9:68.1: “*Sādhu*, friend,” those *bhikkhus*, having approved Venerable Sāriputta’s words and given *anumodanā*, asked Venerable Sāriputta a further question:
mn9:68.2: “Could there be, friends, another way in which a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*]?”
mn9:69.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands *āsava*, understands the arising of *āsava*, understands the cessation of *āsava*, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *āsava*—
mn9:69.3: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].
mn9:7.1: And what, friends, is the root of the skillful?
mn9:7.2: Non-greed is a root of the skillful, non-hatred is a root of the skillful, non-delusion is a root of the skillful—
mn9:7.3: this is called the root of the skillful, friends.
mn9:70.1: And what, friends, is *āsava*, what is the arising of *āsava*, what is the cessation of *āsava*, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of *āsava*?
mn9:70.4: From the arising of blindness [*avijjāsamudayā*] comes the arising of *āsava*, from the cessation of blindness [*avijjānirodhā*] comes the cessation of *āsava*. This very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *āsava*, that is:
mn9:71.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands *āsava*, thus understands the arising of *āsava*, thus understands the cessation of *āsava*, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of *āsava*, he completely abandons the underlying tendency to lust, dispels the underlying tendency to aversion, uproots the underlying tendency to the view and conceit, ‘I am,’ abandons blindness [*avijjaṁ*], brings forth knowledge, and here and now makes an end of dukkha—
mn9:71.2: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].”
mn9:71.3: This is what Venerable Sāriputta said.
mn9:71.4: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* approved Venerable Sāriputta’s words.
mn9:71.5: The Right View is finished, the ninth.
mn9:8.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands the unskillful, thus understands the root of the unskillful, thus understands the skillful, and thus understands the root of the skillful, he completely abandons the underlying tendency to lust, dispels the underlying tendency to aversion, uproots the underlying tendency to the view and conceit, ‘I am,’ abandons blindness [*avijjaṁ*], brings forth knowledge, and here and now makes an end of dukkha—
mn9:8.2: to this extent too, friends, a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*].”
mn9:9.1: “*Sādhu*, friend,” those *bhikkhus*, having approved Venerable Sāriputta’s words and given *anumodanā*, asked Venerable Sāriputta a further question:
mn9:9.2: “Could there be, friends, another way in which a noble disciple has right view, whose view is straight, who is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*, and has come to this true *dhamma* [*saddhammaṁ*]?”
